title: Tables Are Like Spreadsheets
intro: If you've ever used a spreadsheet, you already understand the basics of database tables. Imagine a spreadsheet with strict rules -- you can't put text in a number column or leave required fields blank. Rows, columns, cells -- the concepts are the same. But databases add important rules that spreadsheets don't have. Swipe RIGHT if the statement is true, LEFT if it's false.

teach.title: A Table = A Very Strict Spreadsheet
teach.body: A database table is like a spreadsheet with strict rules. In Google Sheets, you can put anything anywhere -- numbers in a name column, text in an age column. A database table says: 'The name column ONLY accepts text. The age column ONLY accepts numbers. And every row MUST have an ID.' These rules prevent messy, inconsistent data.

teach.title: The Parking Garage
teach.body: A database table is like a well-designed parking garage. Each **floor** is a column type (compact cars, SUVs, motorcycles), each **parking spot** is a cell, and each **parked car** is a row of data. You can't park a semi-truck in a compact spot -- the garage enforces rules about what fits where. And every spot has a unique number so you can always find your car. That's exactly how tables keep data organized and retrievable.

teach.title: Tables, Rows, and Columns
teach.body:
A database table has three parts:

1. **Columns** (also called fields) -- define what KIND of data is stored (name, email, age)
2. **Rows** (also called records) -- each row is one item (one user, one product, one order)
3. **Cells** -- the intersection of a row and column (user #5's email)

A 'users' table might have columns for id, name, email, and created_at, with one row per user.

teach.title: A Table Visualized
teach.annotation: Each column has a name and a data type. Each row is one user. The 'id' column uniquely identifies each row. This looks just like a spreadsheet, but with enforced rules about what data can go in each column.

teach.body: Knowing how tables work is like knowing how to organize inventory in a store. When you vibecode an app with AI, you need to tell it what tables to create. Get this right and your app can handle thousands of paying customers with clean, reliable data.
A database table is like a strict spreadsheet: columns define the type of data, rows hold individual records, and rules ensure data stays clean and consistent.

cards.content: Each row in a database table represents one record (like one user or one product).
cards.explanation: Just like a spreadsheet where each row is one entry, each database row is one complete record. A users table has one row per user, a products table has one row per product, and so on.

cards.content: In a database table, you can put any type of data in any column, just like a spreadsheet.
cards.explanation: Unlike spreadsheets, database columns enforce data types. If a column is defined as INTEGER, you can't put text in it. If it's VARCHAR (text), you can't put a date in it. This strictness prevents data quality problems that are common in spreadsheets.

cards.content: A database can have many tables that are related to each other.
cards.explanation: Most databases have multiple tables. An e-commerce database might have users, products, orders, and reviews tables. These tables can reference each other -- for example, an order row points to which user placed it and which products are in it.

cards.content: You should put all your app's data in a single large table to keep things simple.
cards.explanation: Putting everything in one table creates a mess. Imagine a table with user name, user email, order date, product name, product price all in one row -- you'd repeat the user's name for every order they make. Separate tables for users, orders, and products avoid this repetition and keep data organized.

cards.content: The term 'schema' refers to the structure of your database -- what tables exist and what columns they have.
cards.explanation: A schema is like a blueprint for your database. It defines all the tables, their columns, data types, and rules. When someone asks 'What's the schema?' they want to know the structure -- not the actual data, just the shape of the containers that hold the data.
